5-1.q: Which component of the COSO framework is considered the Foundation of internal control?
o: Risk Assessment
Control Activities
Control Environment
Monitoring Activities
5-1.e: The Control Environment is the foundation of the COSO framework. It sets the tone at the top — ethical values, board oversight, management philosophy, organizational structure, and commitment to competence. Without a strong control environment, the other four components cannot function effectively.

5-1.q: Internal controls provide what level of assurance?
o: Absolute assurance — all errors and fraud will be detected
Reasonable assurance — not absolute
Guaranteed assurance if properly designed
Zero-defect assurance
5-1.e: Internal controls provide Reasonable Assurance, not absolute. Limitations include human error, collusion between employees, and management override. The cost of controls must also be weighed against expected benefits. No control system can eliminate All risk.

5-1.q: Which element of the Fraud Triangle relates to weak internal controls?
o: Pressure
Rationalization
Opportunity
Incentive
5-1.e: Opportunity is the element of the Fraud Triangle linked to weak internal controls. When controls are absent or easy to circumvent, employees have the opportunity to commit fraud. Strong controls (segregation of duties, approvals, reconciliations) directly reduce the opportunity element.

5-1.q: Segregation of Duties requires separation of which four key functions?
o: Planning, Executing, Reviewing, Reporting
Authorization, Recording, Custody, Reconciliation
Budgeting, Variance, Approval, Audit
Purchasing, Receiving, Payment, Reporting
5-1.e: The four duties that must be Separated: (1) Authorization — approving transactions; (2) Recording — entering in accounting system; (3) Custody — physical possession of assets; (4) Reconciliation — comparing records to physical assets. No single person should control all four phases of the same transaction.

5-1.q: A Detective control is one that:
o: Prevents errors from occurring in the first place
Identifies errors or irregularities After they have occurred
Corrects errors once identified
Compensates for other weak controls
5-1.e: Detective controls identify problems After they occur (e.g., bank reconciliations, internal audits, variance analysis). Preventive controls stop problems before they happen (e.g., requiring dual approvals). Preventive is generally preferred, but detective controls are essential as a second line of defense.

5-1.q: How many components does the COSO Internal Control framework have?
o: Three
Four
Five
Seven
5-1.e: The COSO Internal Control — Integrated Framework (2013) has Five components: (1) Control Environment, (2) Risk Assessment, (3) Control Activities, (4) Information & Communication, and (5) Monitoring Activities. All five must be present and integrated for internal control to be effective.

5-1.q: The phrase 'tone at the top' is Most associated with which COSO component?
o: Risk Assessment
Monitoring Activities
Control Activities
Control Environment
5-1.e: 'Tone at the top' refers to the ethical culture, values, and leadership set by senior management and the board — these are all core elements of the Control Environment, the foundation of COSO. If tone at the top is weak, the entire control structure is compromised regardless of how many procedures exist.

5-1.q: Which COSO component ensures that relevant information is captured and shared with the right people in a timely manner?
o: Monitoring Activities
Control Activities
Information & Communication
Risk Assessment
5-1.e: Information & Communication (4th COSO component): relevant information must be identified, captured, and communicated in a timely manner so people can carry out their responsibilities. Both internal (policies, procedures, reports) and external (regulatory, customer) communications are included.

5-1.q: A Compensating control is Best described as:
o: A control that prevents errors before they happen
A control that detects errors after they happen
An Alternative control used when the primary control is not feasible
A control that corrects errors once found
5-1.e: A Compensating Control is an alternative that mitigates risk when the preferred control (e.g., segregation of duties) is not possible — often due to limited staff in small companies. Example: if one person handles both cash and records, a manager reviews all transactions as a compensating control.

5-1.q: Which COSO component includes reconciliations, authorizations, and physical safeguards?
o: Control Environment
Risk Assessment
Monitoring Activities
Control Activities
5-1.e: Control Activities (3rd component) are the specific Policies And Procedures that ensure management directives are carried out and risks are mitigated. Examples: authorizations, reconciliations, physical controls, performance reviews, IT controls, and segregation of duties.

5-1.q: Which limitation of internal controls occurs when employees work together to defeat a control?
o: Management override
Human error
Collusion
Cost-benefit trade-off
5-1.e: Collusion occurs when two or more employees cooperate to circumvent controls. For example, if one employee authorizes transactions and another records them, their collusion defeats the segregation of duties control. Collusion is one of the key inherent limitations of internal control that prevents absolute assurance.

5-1.q: Management override of controls is a significant risk because:
o: It is easy to detect through standard controls
It represents misuse of authority by those who designed the controls — very hard to prevent
It only affects small companies
External auditors always catch it
5-1.e: Management Override is particularly dangerous because managers have the authority and knowledge to bypass the very controls they implemented. It is the primary mechanism for financial statement fraud. This is why independent board oversight, audit committees, external audits, and whistleblower programs are critical — they provide checks on management itself.

5-1.q: A padlocked supply room requiring badge access to enter is an example of which type of control?
o: Detective control
Corrective control
Authorization control
Physical control — a type of preventive control
5-1.e: Physical Controls protect assets from unauthorized access, theft, or damage. They are Preventive in nature. Examples: locked safes, badge access systems, security cameras, fenced storage yards. They directly reduce the Opportunity element of the Fraud Triangle.

5-1.q: The COSO Monitoring component differs from Control Activities because monitoring:
o: Establishes initial control procedures
Evaluates Whether the other controls continue to operate effectively over time
Identifies risks to the organization
Communicates information to stakeholders
5-1.e: Monitoring Activities (5th component) evaluate whether the other four COSO components are present and functioning over time. Controls can deteriorate — staff change, processes evolve, new risks emerge. Monitoring catches this deterioration. Control Activities, by contrast, are the actual policies and procedures themselves.

5-1.q: In the context of the Fraud Triangle, Rationalization refers to:
o: The financial pressure felt by the fraudster
The opportunity created by weak controls
The fraudster's mental Justification for committing fraud
The capability to commit and conceal fraud
5-1.e: Rationalization is the mental process by which the fraudster justifies their actions: 'I deserve this raise,' 'I'll pay it back,' 'The company won't miss it,' 'Everyone does it.' Organizations can reduce rationalization through a strong ethical culture, fair compensation, and a zero-tolerance fraud policy.

5-1.q: Which of the following Best illustrates a violation of Segregation of Duties?
o: A manager reviews and approves all purchase orders over $5,000
The same employee authorizes purchases And signs the payment checks
The warehouse manager counts inventory and submits the count to accounting
An IT administrator has read-only access to financial records
5-1.e: Having the same person Authorize purchases And sign checks combines authorization and custody/disbursement — two duties that must be separated. This creates opportunity: the employee could authorize fictitious purchases and then pay themselves. Sod requires separating: Authorization, Recording, Custody, and Reconciliation.

5-1.q: Internal controls are designed to provide assurance regarding which Three objectives?
o: Profitability, liquidity, and solvency
Operations, financial reporting, and compliance
Revenue, expenses, and cash flows
Internal audit, external audit, and management review
5-1.e: The COSO framework addresses three categories of objectives: (1) Operations — effective and efficient use of resources; (2) Financial Reporting — reliable financial and non-financial reporting; (3) Compliance — adherence to applicable laws and regulations. Internal controls provide reasonable assurance for all three categories.

5-1.q: A Corrective control is Best illustrated by:
o: Requiring dual approval before payment
Running a report to identify duplicate invoices
Issuing an adjusting journal entry to fix an identified accounting error
Installing a firewall to block unauthorized access
5-1.e: Corrective controls Fix problems after they have been detected. Issuing an adjusting entry corrects an error that was already found. Dual approval = preventive. Duplicate invoice report = detective. Firewall = preventive. The sequence is: Preventive (stop it) → Detective (find it) → Corrective (fix it).

5-1.q: Which principle states that the cost of implementing a control should not exceed the benefit it provides?
o: Materiality principle
Going concern principle
Cost-benefit constraint
Conservatism principle
5-1.e: The Cost-Benefit Constraint means controls should only be implemented when their benefits (risk reduction, error prevention, fraud deterrence) exceed their costs (implementation, maintenance, employee time). Not every theoretical risk warrants a control — management must prioritize based on likelihood, impact, and cost.

5-1.q: In a small company with only one accountant, the Best compensating control for the inability to segregate duties is:
o: Allowing the accountant to also approve journal entries
Having the owner/manager independently review all bank statements and transactions
Not implementing any controls until the company grows
Eliminating cash transactions
5-1.e: When true Sod is not possible due to limited staff, the Owner Or Senior Manager independently reviewing bank statements, cancelled checks, and reconciliations is the most effective compensating control. It provides oversight by someone outside the accounting function and can detect unauthorized transactions or manipulated records.

5-1.q: The COSO 2013 framework contains how many principles across its five components?
o: 5 principles — one per component
10 principles — two per component
17 principles — supporting all five components
25 principles — five per component
5-1.e: COSO 2013 contains 17 Principles distributed across the five components: Control Environment (5), Risk Assessment (4), Control Activities (3), Information & Communication (3), Monitoring Activities (2). All 17 must be present and functioning for internal control to be considered effective. The Ima exam tests specific principles, particularly within the Control Environment and Risk Assessment components.

5-1.q: Principle 8 of the COSO framework requires management to specifically assess:
o: IT system security risks
Operational efficiency risks
Fraud Risk — the possibility that fraud could prevent achievement of objectives
Market and credit risks
5-1.e: COSO Principle 8 (within Risk Assessment): management must explicitly assess Fraud Risk — the risk that fraud could prevent the organization from achieving its objectives. This includes assessing incentive/pressure, opportunity, and attitude/rationalization factors. A separate, explicit fraud risk assessment is required — it cannot be embedded informally in general risk assessment. This principle directly links to the Fraud Triangle.

5-2.q: Residual risk is best defined as:
o: The risk before any controls are in place
The risk Remaining after management has responded to the risk with controls
The maximum possible loss from a risk event
The risk that auditors fail to detect errors
5-2.e: Residual Risk = Inherent Risk After applying controls and risk responses. If inherent risk is high and controls are strong, residual risk is low. Organizations set a target residual risk level aligned with their risk appetite. Residual risk should be monitored continuously.

5-2.q: Risk Appetite is defined as:
o: The maximum loss the company can absorb
The amount and type of risk an organization is Willing to accept to achieve its objectives — set by the board
The risk that remains after controls are applied
The risk response strategy chosen by management
5-2.e: Risk Appetite = the amount and Type of risk an organization is willing to pursue/accept in pursuit of objectives. It is set by the Board and reflects the organization's strategy and values. Risk appetite guides decisions about which risks to take and which to avoid or mitigate.

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy involves insurance or outsourcing?
o: Avoid
Reduce
Accept
Share/Transfer
5-2.e: Share/Transfer: The risk is shifted to a third party — through insurance (transfer financial impact), outsourcing (transfer operational risk), hedging (transfer financial risk), or joint ventures. The risk is not eliminated, but the financial consequences are borne by another party.

5-2.q: Enterprise Risk Management (Erm) differs from traditional internal control because it:
o: Focuses only on financial reporting risk
Also addresses Strategic risks and value creation, not just operational and compliance risks
Is only relevant for large companies
Replaces the need for internal controls
5-2.e: Erm is Broader — it addresses All risks including strategic, operational, financial, and compliance, and links risk management to Strategy and Value Creation. Traditional COSO Ic focuses on controls for financial reporting and compliance. Erm considers the entire risk portfolio and how it affects the organization's ability to achieve its mission.

5-2.q: Control risk refers to:
o: The risk that an error will occur in a transaction
The risk that the company's internal controls Fail to prevent or detect a material misstatement
The risk that auditors fail to find an error
The inherent risk in a process before controls
5-2.e: Control Risk = the risk that internal controls Fail to prevent or detect a material error or fraud. High control risk exists when controls are weak, absent, or not operating effectively. Auditors assess control risk to determine how much substantive testing is needed.

5-2.q: Inherent risk is Best defined as:
o: Risk remaining after management applies controls
Risk that auditors fail to detect errors
The level of risk that Exists Before any controls are applied
Risk related to the entity's business environment only
5-2.e: Inherent Risk = the susceptibility of an account or assertion to material misstatement Before considering any related internal controls. Some accounts are inherently riskier than others (cash is more inherently risky than prepaid expenses). Inherent risk is the Starting Point — then controls reduce it to residual risk.

5-2.q: If a company has high inherent risk and effective controls, the residual risk will be:
o: Equal to inherent risk
Higher than inherent risk
Low — strong controls significantly reduce the inherent risk
Unrelated to inherent risk
5-2.e: Residual Risk = Inherent Risk Minus the effect of controls. If inherent risk is High but controls are Strong and effective, residual risk can be Low. This is the goal of risk management: identify areas of high inherent risk and implement proportionately strong controls to bring residual risk within acceptable bounds.

5-2.q: Risk Tolerance differs from risk Appetite in that risk tolerance is:
o: Set by the board at the strategic level
The Acceptable Variation around the risk appetite — the operational range management works within
The maximum possible loss from any risk event
Identical to risk appetite in practice
5-2.e: Risk Appetite = the broad level of risk the organization will accept (set by the board). Risk Tolerance = the acceptable variation Around that appetite — the operational boundaries management uses day-to-day. Example: risk appetite = 'moderate credit risk'; risk tolerance = 'no more than 5% of receivables over 90 days.'

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy involves Eliminating the risk by not engaging in the activity at all?
o: Accept
Reduce
Share/Transfer
Avoid
5-2.e: Avoid: The organization eliminates the risk by not engaging in the risky activity. Example: a company stops offering a product line that creates significant legal liability. Most extreme and costly response — gives up potential benefits. Used when the risk is too high relative to any potential benefit.

5-2.q: Which risk response strategy is Most appropriate when the cost of mitigating a risk exceeds the expected benefit?
o: Avoid the risk entirely
Share the risk through insurance
Accept the risk — acknowledge it and do nothing (when cost > benefit)
Reduce the risk with additional controls
5-2.e: Accept (or Retain): The organization acknowledges the risk and takes no action — typically when the cost of mitigation exceeds the expected benefit, or when the residual risk is within the risk appetite. This is a conscious management decision, not neglect. It should be documented and monitored.

5-2.q: In the Audit Risk model, detection risk refers to:
o: The risk that controls fail to prevent material misstatements
The risk an account is misstated before controls
The risk that Auditors' procedures fail to find a material error that exists
The risk that management overrides controls
5-2.e: Detection Risk: The risk that Audit Procedures will fail to detect a material misstatement that exists. It is the only element of the audit risk model that auditors can Control by designing more rigorous procedures. Audit Risk = Inherent Risk × Control Risk × Detection Risk. When inherent and control risk are high, auditors lower detection risk by doing more extensive testing.

5-2.q: COSO Erm (2017) addresses which type of risk that the original COSO Ic framework does Not explicitly address?
o: Compliance risk
Operational risk
Strategic risk — risks related to achieving the organization's mission and strategy
Financial reporting risk
5-2.e: COSO Erm (2017) explicitly links risk management to Strategy and Value Creation — addressing strategic risks that could prevent the organization from achieving its mission. The original COSO Ic (2013) focuses on operations, financial reporting, and compliance but does not directly link to strategy. Erm is broader and more forward-looking.

5-2.q: Risk Threshold is Best defined as:
o: The amount of risk the board is willing to accept
The acceptable variation around risk appetite
The Level At Which Risk Becomes Unacceptable and triggers escalation or action
The maximum insurance coverage available
5-2.e: Risk Threshold = the specific level at which risk becomes unacceptable and triggers a mandatory response or escalation to senior management. It is the 'red line' beyond which management must act. Example: if customer concentration threshold = 30% revenue from one client, exceeding this triggers a mitigation plan.

5-2.q: The Governance & Culture component of COSO Erm (2017) is responsible for:
o: Identifying specific operational risks
Setting the Tone, risk appetite, and risk culture from leadership
Running day-to-day risk controls
Testing the effectiveness of risk responses
5-2.e: In COSO Erm (2017), Governance & Culture is the first component — it establishes the foundation: board oversight of Erm, organizational risk culture, the risk appetite, ethical values, and management's commitment to risk management. Without proper governance and culture, all other Erm components are ineffective.

5-2.q: A manufacturing company purchases insurance to cover potential product liability claims. This is an example of which risk response?
o: Avoid
Reduce
Share/Transfer — shifting the financial impact to the insurer
Accept
5-2.e: Share/Transfer: Insurance shifts the Financial Consequences of a risk to a third party (the insurer). The underlying risk still exists — the company still makes products that could injure people — but the financial impact is transferred. Other examples: outsourcing IT operations (transfers operational risk), currency hedging (transfers exchange rate risk).

5-2.q: Which of the following is an example of a Strategic risk?
o: An employee inputs an incorrect invoice amount
A fire destroys the warehouse
A competitor launches a superior product that makes the company's product obsolete
A vendor is paid twice for the same invoice
5-2.e: Strategic Risks threaten the organization's ability to achieve its strategic objectives. A disruptive competitor is a classic strategic risk — it affects the core business model. Incorrect invoice entry = operational risk. Fire = operational/hazard risk. Duplicate payment = operational/control risk. Erm addresses all types including strategic.

5-2.q: In Erm, the relationship between risk appetite and strategy means that:
o: Strategy is set first, then risk appetite is aligned to it
The board sets risk appetite First, and strategy choices must stay within that appetite
Risk appetite is irrelevant to strategic decisions
Risk appetite only applies after a loss event
5-2.e: In COSO Erm (2017), the Board sets the risk Appetite first (reflecting the organization's values and stakeholder expectations), then management develops Strategies that operate within that appetite. Strategies that would require accepting risk beyond the appetite must be modified or rejected. Risk appetite and strategy are tightly linked.

5-2.q: A company identifies that a new regulation will significantly increase compliance costs. This risk should First be:
o: Immediately shared through insurance
Avoided by exiting the industry
Identified And Assessed — evaluate likelihood and impact before choosing a response
Accepted without any action
5-2.e: The Erm process: Identify the risk, then Assess it (likelihood × impact), then choose a Response. Jumping to a response without assessment leads to over- or under-investment in controls. A compliance risk from new regulation may be mitigated by building internal processes, partially transferred through legal insurance, or in some cases avoided by product/market repositioning.

5-2.q: Control risk is High when:
o: Internal controls are well-designed and operating effectively
Inherent risk in the account is low
Internal Controls Are Weak or absent — they cannot prevent or detect material misstatements
The external auditor has extensive experience with the client
5-2.e: High Control Risk means internal controls are Weak or Not Functioning — they provide little protection against material misstatements. Auditors respond to high control risk by increasing substantive testing (lowering detection risk). In a well-controlled environment, control risk is low and auditors can reduce the extent of substantive testing.

5-2.q: Risk assessment in the COSO framework requires management to consider risks related to:
o: Only financial statement fraud
Both internal and external sources of risk that could affect the achievement of objectives
Only regulatory compliance
Only operational disruptions
5-2.e: COSO Risk Assessment requires management to identify and analyze risks from Both Internal sources (system failures, employee turnover, management override, process changes) and External sources (economic changes, competition, regulation, technology disruption, natural disasters). All risks that could threaten the three categories of objectives (operations, reporting, compliance) must be considered.

5-3.q: The Three-Way Match in accounts payable involves matching:
o: Purchase order, vendor invoice, and payment check
Purchase order, receiving report, and vendor invoice
Budget, actual, and variance
Sales order, delivery note, and customer invoice
5-3.e: Three-Way Match: (1) Purchase Order — what was authorized to buy; (2) Receiving Report — what was actually received; (3) Vendor Invoice — what the vendor is billing. All three must agree before payment is made. This control prevents paying for goods not ordered or not received.

5-3.q: Which of the following is a Preventive control?
o: Monthly bank reconciliation
Variance analysis report
Requiring dual signatures on checks above $10,000
Annual internal audit
5-3.e: Requiring Dual Signatures is Preventive — it stops unauthorized payments Before they occur by requiring two approvals. Bank reconciliations (detective — finds errors after), variance analysis (detective — identifies deviations after), and internal audits (detective/monitoring) all act After the fact.

5-3.q: A budget-to-actual variance analysis is an example of a:
o: Preventive control — it stops errors before they happen
Detective control — it identifies deviations after they occur
Corrective control — it fixes the budget
Authorization control — it approves expenditures
5-3.e: Variance analysis is a Detective control — it compares actual results to budget After the period ends and identifies significant deviations that may indicate errors, fraud, or inefficiencies. It cannot prevent errors, but it can detect them and trigger investigation.

5-3.q: Physical controls over assets are designed primarily to:
o: Detect misstatements in financial records
Prevent unauthorized access, use, or theft of assets
Correct errors in accounting records
Test the accuracy of financial reports
5-3.e: Physical Controls protect assets from unauthorized access, theft, or misuse. Examples: locked safes, security cameras, badge access, physical inventory counts. They are Preventive in nature — they stop the unauthorized use before it happens (or deter it). Financial records cannot substitute for physical protection of assets.

5-3.q: Soft controls (informal controls) are best described as:
o: IT system access restrictions and written approval policies
Controls related to Organizational Culture, ethics, and management integrity
Physical security systems
Documented reconciliation procedures
5-3.e: Soft Controls relate to the human and cultural elements: ethical culture, tone at the top, management integrity, and employee competence/values. They are harder to observe and test than hard controls (formal policies, system restrictions) but are critically important. The Fraud Triangle shows that rationalization (a soft-control failure) is a key fraud driver.

5-3.q: Authorization controls are designed to ensure that:
o: All transactions are recorded accurately
Only Approved, Legitimate transactions are initiated and processed
Cash is always deposited daily
Reconciliations are completed monthly
5-3.e: Authorization Controls ensure that only Approved transactions occur — they prevent unauthorized activity by requiring management sign-off, approval levels (e.g., only the CFO can approve payments over $50,000), or system-enforced rules. They are Preventive controls — they stop unauthorized transactions before they happen.

5-3.q: A Reconciliation is Best classified as which type of control?
o: Preventive — it stops errors before they happen
Detective — it identifies discrepancies after transactions have occurred
Corrective — it fixes errors once found
Authorization — it approves transactions
5-3.e: Reconciliation is a Detective control — it compares two independent sources of information After transactions have been recorded to identify differences. Examples: bank reconciliation (bank statement vs. general ledger), physical inventory count vs. inventory records. It cannot prevent errors but identifies them for investigation and correction.

5-3.q: The Three-Way Match control in accounts payable Primarily prevents:
o: Employee payroll fraud
Paying for goods that were Never Ordered or Never Received
Budget overruns
Incorrect depreciation calculations
5-3.e: Three-Way Match (Purchase Order + Receiving Report + Vendor Invoice) prevents: (1) paying for goods not authorized (no Po), (2) paying for goods not received (no receiving report), (3) paying wrong amounts (invoice doesn't match Po). It is the primary preventive control over the purchase-to-pay cycle.

5-3.q: In a payroll system, which control Best prevents the payment of 'ghost employees'?
o: Monthly payroll bank reconciliation
Requiring HR To Independently Authorize all new hires and pay rates, separate from payroll preparation
Having the payroll clerk also verify employment
Reconciling payroll to the general ledger quarterly
5-3.e: 'Ghost employees' are fictitious employees added to payroll by a dishonest employee. The control: HR independently authorizes All new hires and pay rate changes, Separate from payroll preparation and payment. This segregates the custody (payroll payments) from authorization (HR). Any new payee not in HR records should trigger immediate investigation.

5-3.q: A Surprise Cash Count of a cashier's drawer is an example of which type of control?
o: Preventive — it stops theft before it happens
Detective — it identifies discrepancies that may indicate theft or error
Corrective — it fixes missing cash
Authorization — it approves the cash balance
5-3.e: A Surprise cash count is Detective — it discovers whether the actual cash on hand matches the recorded balance, after the fact. The 'surprise' element means the cashier cannot prepare or manipulate cash before the count. It deters theft (since the cashier knows a surprise count could happen at any time) but detects rather than prevents.

5-3.q: Which of the following represents an Application of physical controls over fixed assets?
o: Approving capital expenditures before purchase
Recording depreciation monthly
A Periodic Physical Inventory Of Assets, comparing assets found to the fixed asset register
Reconciling the fixed asset register to the general ledger
5-3.e: Physical Controls over fixed assets include: (1) periodic physical inventory — verifying that recorded assets actually exist; (2) asset tagging (Id numbers); (3) secure storage of high-value assets; (4) access restrictions for sensitive equipment. Periodic physical inventory is a Detective physical control — it compares what should be there (records) to what is actually there.

5-3.q: Hard controls differ from soft controls in that hard controls are:
o: Related to culture and ethics
Based on management's personal values
Formal, Documented controls — written policies, system access restrictions, approval workflows — that are observable and testable
Only effective in large organizations
5-3.e: Hard Controls (formal controls) are tangible, documented, and easily tested: written policies, system access rights, approval workflows, reconciliation procedures, physical locks. Soft Controls are informal: organizational culture, ethical tone, management integrity, employee competence. Both are essential — weak soft controls (rationalization) drive fraud even when hard controls exist.

5-3.q: A performance review comparing actual costs to budget is Primarily useful as:
o: A preventive control — it stops cost overruns
A Detective control — it identifies significant variances that may indicate errors or fraud after the fact
A corrective control — it adjusts the budget
An authorization control — it approves expenditures
5-3.e: Budget-vs.-actual variance analysis is Detective — it identifies significant deviations after the period ends. Large unexplained variances can signal errors (miscoding transactions), fraud (fictitious expenses), or operational problems. Management should investigate material variances promptly. It cannot prevent the underlying errors, but it can catch them before they compound.

5-3.q: A data entry edit check that Rejects a customer order if the customer credit limit would be exceeded is an example of:
o: A detective control — finding errors after they occur
A corrective control — fixing identified credit problems
A Preventive Application Input Control — stopping an unauthorized transaction before it is processed
A physical control — protecting assets
5-3.e: Edit checks in IT applications are Preventive Application Controls — they automatically validate input data Before processing and Reject invalid entries. Examples: credit limit checks (reject orders exceeding limit), format checks (date must be Mm/Dd/Yyyy), range checks (quantity must be 1–999), completeness checks (required fields cannot be blank).

5-3.q: Which control is Most effective for preventing Duplicate Payments to vendors?
o: Requiring two signatures on all checks
System-Based Duplicate Invoice Detection — checking vendor/invoice number/amount combination before payment
Monthly bank reconciliation
Mailing checks directly to vendors
5-3.e: System-based Duplicate Detection checks each invoice against previously paid invoices for the same vendor number, invoice number, and/or amount before payment is processed — Preventing the duplicate payment. Monthly bank reconciliation would only Detect it afterward. Two signatures prevent unauthorized payments but not duplicates. Direct mailing prevents check theft but not duplicates.

5-3.q: The Most critical preventive control in the Cash Receipts cycle is:
o: Monthly bank reconciliation
Depositing all receipts daily and using restrictive endorsements on checks
Recording cash when received
Reconciling accounts receivable quarterly
5-3.e: In the cash receipts cycle, the most critical preventive controls are: (1) Daily Deposits — cash sits in the company for minimum time, reducing theft opportunity; (2) Restrictive Endorsements ('For deposit only — [Company Name]') on checks immediately upon receipt — making them difficult to divert; (3) prenumbered receipts. Bank reconciliation is a detective control.

5-3.q: Which of the following Best describes an information processing control?
o: Requiring manager approval before recording a large journal entry
A physical lock on the server room
A System Run-To-Run Total that verifies record counts processed match records submitted
Comparing the fixed asset register to physical assets
5-3.e: Information Processing Controls ensure completeness and accuracy of data processing. Run-To-Run Totals compare the count/sum of records entering a processing step to records exiting it — any difference indicates records were lost, duplicated, or altered during processing. Manager approval = authorization. Server lock = physical. Fixed asset check = reconciliation/physical.

5-3.q: In the context of purchasing controls, Separating the purchasing function from the Payment function is designed to prevent:
o: Errors in recording invoices
Fraudulent Schemes where an employee could order goods for personal use and then authorize payment
Vendors from submitting duplicate invoices
Budget overruns in the procurement department
5-3.e: Separating Purchasing (who can create purchase orders/authorize purchases) from Payment (who signs checks or approves electronic transfers) implements Sod and prevents an employee from: creating fictitious purchase orders for personal purchases And approving the payment to themselves or a shell company. This is a fundamental preventive control in the procure-to-pay cycle.

5-3.q: Soft controls are Most directly linked to which element of the Fraud Triangle?
o: Pressure — financial need
Opportunity — weak procedures
Rationalization — the ethical culture that makes fraud seem justifiable or unjustifiable
Detection — audit procedures
5-3.e: Soft Controls (ethical culture, management integrity, fairness, employee satisfaction) directly reduce Rationalization — the third element of the Fraud Triangle. When employees feel fairly treated, see ethical leadership, and believe wrongdoing will be taken seriously, they find it harder to rationalize fraud. Hard controls primarily address Opportunity; employment pressure factors address Pressure.

5-3.q: A company's control requires that all journal entries over $10,000 be approved by the Controller before posting. This control is Best classified as:
o: Detective — finds unauthorized entries after the fact
Corrective — fixes incorrect journal entries
Preventive Authorization Control — stops unauthorized journal entries from being recorded
Physical control — protects financial records
5-3.e: Requiring Controller Approval before posting large journal entries is a Preventive Authorization Control. It stops unauthorized or erroneous journal entries from entering the general ledger in the first place. This is particularly important because unauthorized journal entries are a common financial statement fraud technique (Acfe research consistently shows fraudulent journal entries as a top scheme).

5-4.q: General IT Controls (Gitc) differ from application controls because Gitc:
o: Apply only to payroll systems
Apply to the Overall IT Environment and support all application controls
Are only tested during year-end audits
Control only data input validation
5-4.e: Gitc (General IT Controls) apply to the Overall IT environment — they are the foundation for application controls. If Gitc are weak (e.g., poor access controls, no change management), application controls built on top may also be unreliable. Examples: logical access security, change management, backup procedures.

5-4.q: Which control is designed to ensure only Authorized users access the accounting system?
o: Output controls
Batch totals
Logical access controls (user IDs, passwords, role-based access)
Run-to-run totals
5-4.e: Logical Access Controls restrict system access to authorized users. These include user IDs, strong passwords, multi-factor authentication, and role-based access (users can only access what their job requires). This is a fundamental preventive IT control aligned with the Confidentiality principle of the CIA triad.

5-4.q: The CIA Triad in information security stands for:
o: Control, Integrity, Authorization
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Compliance, Investigation, Assurance
Custody, Independence, Accountability
5-4.e: CIA Triad: (1) Confidentiality — data accessible only to authorized users (controls: encryption, access restrictions); (2) Integrity — data is accurate and not tampered with (controls: audit trails, hash functions); (3) Availability — systems accessible when needed (controls: backups, redundancy, disaster recovery). All three must be balanced.

5-4.q: In IT segregation of duties, who should Not also be the computer operator?
o: Data entry clerk
The external auditor
The Programmer — writing code and operating the system creates incompatible duties
The internal auditor
5-4.e: The Programmer and the Computer Operator should be Separated. If the same person writes/modifies code And operates the system day-to-day, they could make unauthorized program changes and then process transactions to cover fraud. Key IT Sod separations: Analyst ≠ Programmer ≠ Operator ≠ Data Entry.

5-4.q: An Input control in an accounting application that rejects a transaction if the date is more than 30 days old is an example of a:
o: Batch total control
Validity / Reasonableness Check — prevents processing of illogical data
Output distribution control
Change management control
5-4.e: Validity / Reasonableness Check is an application input control that tests whether data is logically valid (e.g., date within acceptable range, amount within expected limits). It prevents processing of stale, erroneous, or fraudulent input. Other input controls: format checks (field type), completeness checks (required fields), range checks (amounts within bounds).

5-4.q: Change management controls in IT are designed to:
o: Allow programmers to make system updates at any time
Ensure that changes to programs and systems are authorized, tested, and documented before implementation
Monitor user access to financial data
Back up data on a regular schedule
5-4.e: Change Management Controls govern the entire software development/change lifecycle: changes must be (1) requested and Authorized; (2) tested in a non-production environment; (3) reviewed for security impacts; (4) documented; (5) approved before promotion to production. Without these controls, unauthorized or untested code changes can introduce errors, backdoors, or fraud mechanisms.

5-4.q: Role-Based Access Control (Rbac) assigns system access based on:
o: The employee's seniority and years of service
The employee's Job Function — users receive only the access needed for their specific role
The employee's personal preferences
A fixed access level for all employees in a department
5-4.e: Rbac implements the Least Privilege principle — users get Only the access their job requires, no more. An accounts payable clerk should access AP functions only, not payroll or general ledger setup. This limits the damage a compromised account or dishonest employee can cause and implements IT segregation of duties automatically through system permissions.

5-4.q: A Batch Total is an example of which type of IT application control?
o: Output control
Processing Control — verifying that all records in a batch are processed correctly
Input control — validating data before entry
Change management control
5-4.e: Batch Totals are Processing Controls: before processing, a control total (sum of amounts, count of records) is calculated. After processing, the same total is recalculated. If they don't match, records were lost, duplicated, or altered during processing. Also called run-to-run totals or hash totals. They ensure the Completeness and Accuracy of processing.

5-4.q: Under the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud computing, the cloud Provider is responsible for:
o: Securing the customer's data and applications
Securing The Underlying Infrastructure — hardware, hypervisor, physical facilities, and network
Setting the customer's access permissions
Encrypting the customer's sensitive data
5-4.e: In the Shared Responsibility Model: the Cloud Provider secures the physical infrastructure, hypervisors, and global network. The Customer is responsible for data security, application security, access management, and encryption of their own data. This division varies by service type (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). Many cloud data breaches occur because the customer fails to properly secure Their side of the model.

5-4.q: Encryption primarily protects which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Availability — systems are accessible when needed
Integrity — data has not been tampered with
Confidentiality — only authorized users can read the encrypted data
All three equally
5-4.e: Encryption primarily protects Confidentiality: it transforms data into unreadable ciphertext so only those with the decryption key can read it. If encrypted data is intercepted (e.g., in transit on a network), it is useless to the attacker. Hash functions and digital signatures protect Integrity. Redundancy and backups protect Availability.

5-4.q: An Audit Trail in an accounting system primarily protects which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Confidentiality — restricts who can see data
Availability — ensures systems are accessible
Integrity — provides a record of who changed what data and when
Performance — speeds up transaction processing
5-4.e: Audit Trails protect Integrity — they record every change to data: who made it, when, what the before and after values were. This deters unauthorized changes (people know there's a record) and enables detection and investigation of tampering. Audit trails are a critical IT control for financial systems where data integrity is paramount.

5-4.q: Multi-Factor Authentication (Mfa) strengthens access control by requiring:
o: A very long password of at least 16 characters
Two Or More Verification Factors from different categories: something you know + something you have + something you are
All employees to share a single login credential
Daily password changes
5-4.e: Mfa requires verification from multiple categories: (1) Something You Know — password/Pin; (2) Something You Have — phone/token/smart card; (3) Something You Are — fingerprint/facial recognition. Even if a password is stolen, the attacker cannot access the system without the second factor. Mfa significantly reduces the risk of unauthorized access from phishing or credential theft.

5-4.q: A Soc 2 report provides assurance about a Service Organization'S controls related to:
o: Financial statement accuracy only
Tax compliance
Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and/or Privacy of systems
The service organization's revenue recognition policies
5-4.e: Soc 2 (System and Organization Controls 2) reports provide third-party assurance on a service organization's controls related to the Trust Service Criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. User entities (customers) rely on Soc 2 reports to assess the controls of cloud providers, SaaS vendors, and other IT service providers without conducting their own audits.

5-4.q: Output controls in an IT system are designed to ensure that:
o: Only valid data is entered into the system
All records are processed completely and accurately
Reports And Output Are Complete, Accurate, And Distributed Only To Authorized Recipients
Programs are tested before deployment
5-4.e: Output Controls ensure the final products of processing: (1) Completeness — all records are included in output; (2) Accuracy — output correctly reflects the processed data; (3) Distribution — reports go only to authorized people who need them; (4) Retention — output is kept for the required period. Weak output controls can lead to sensitive information reaching unauthorized people.

5-4.q: A Firewall primarily protects against which IT threat?
o: Internal employee data entry errors
Data Loss after hardware failure
Unauthorized External Network Access — filtering traffic between the network and the internet
Corruption of data by authorized users
5-4.e: A Firewall is a network security device that Monitors and Controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules. It creates a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks (internet), blocking unauthorized access attempts while allowing legitimate traffic. Firewalls primarily protect Confidentiality and network security, not internal data entry errors.

5-4.q: In IT segregation of duties, which two roles are Most Important to separate?
o: Data entry and report generation
Systems analyst and data entry
Programmer and Computer Operator — to prevent unauthorized code changes that manipulate transactions
Network administrator and database administrator
5-4.e: Programmer + Computer Operator is the most critical IT Sod separation. A programmer with operational access could: write fraudulent code And execute it in production And process transactions. This complete control enables systematic fraud that is difficult to detect. The analyst designs, the programmer codes, the operator runs — Separate people for each function.

5-4.q: A Range Check as an input control would reject a transaction if:
o: The transaction date is in the wrong format
A required field is left blank
The amount entered is outside the Expected Acceptable Range (e.g., negative quantity or impossibly large amount)
The user does not have the required access level
5-4.e: Range Checks test whether numeric input falls within predefined acceptable boundaries. Example: a quantity field that accepts only 1–9,999; a date that must be within the current fiscal year; a discount rate that cannot exceed 50%. They prevent obviously incorrect or fraudulent values from entering the system. Format check = structure. Completeness check = required fields. Access control = authorization.

5-4.q: Backup and recovery procedures Primarily protect which element of the CIA Triad?
o: Confidentiality — who can see the data
Integrity — accuracy of data
Availability — ensuring data and systems can be restored and accessed when needed
Performance — speed of data access
5-4.e: Backups primarily protect Availability — if data is lost due to hardware failure, ransomware, or disaster, backups enable Restoration of systems and data. Without adequate backups, even a minor disaster could result in permanent data loss. Backup procedures should be tested regularly (restore tests) to confirm they actually work when needed.

5-4.q: The Primary purpose of Logical Access Controls over a financial application is to:
o: Speed up transaction processing
Restrict Access to the system to only Authorized users and ensure they can only perform functions their job requires
Prevent physical theft of computers
Test the accuracy of accounting calculations
5-4.e: Logical Access Controls (passwords, Mfa, role-based access, user IDs) are the primary IT control ensuring only Authorized users access financial systems and that their access is Limited to what their job requires (least privilege). They implement IT segregation of duties by system design and are the foundation of IT security for financial applications.

5-4.q: Intrusion Detection Systems (Ids) in IT security are an example of which type of control?
o: Preventive — they stop attacks before they happen
Detective — they Identify and alert on suspicious or malicious network activity as it occurs
Corrective — they fix damage caused by attacks
Authorization — they approve network traffic
5-4.e: Ids are Detective controls — they Monitor network or system activity for suspicious patterns and Alert security staff when potential intrusions are detected. They do not block attacks (that is a firewall/Ips function) — they identify them for response. Intrusion Prevention Systems (Ips) actively block suspicious traffic and are more preventive in nature.

5-4.q: Cobit (Control Objectives for Information and Related Technologies) is Best described as:
o: A US government regulation for cybersecurity
A set of accounting standards for software companies
An IT Governance Framework developed by Isaca that aligns IT with business objectives and manages IT risks
A replacement for the COSO internal control framework
5-4.e: Cobit is an IT Governance framework developed by Isaca. It provides a comprehensive framework for enterprise IT governance and management — ensuring IT supports business strategy, resources are used responsibly, and IT risks are managed. Cobit is Complementary to COSO (not a replacement) — COSO covers the entire organization; Cobit extends that specifically for IT governance and control.

5-4.q: Cobit and COSO are Best described as:
o: Competing frameworks — organizations must choose one or the other
Identical frameworks with different names
Complementary frameworks — COSO provides the organizational internal control foundation; Cobit extends it specifically for IT governance
Government regulations that replace professional judgment
5-4.e: Cobit and COSO are Complementary: COSO provides the broad organizational internal control framework (5 components, 17 principles) covering operations, financial reporting, and compliance. Cobit maps to COSO and extends it specifically for the IT governance environment — providing detailed control objectives for IT strategy, acquisition, delivery, and monitoring. Organizations use both: COSO as the foundation, Cobit for IT-specific governance.

5-4.q: The Primary distinction between Cobit and Itil is that Cobit focuses on:
o: How to deliver IT services to end users
Day-to-day IT operations management
IT Governance And Control — what IT should accomplish and how it is governed; Itil focuses on how IT services are delivered
Hardware and network infrastructure standards
5-4.e: Cobit vs Itil: Cobit = IT Governance — addresses What IT should do: how IT aligns with business strategy, how IT risks are managed, how IT investments are governed. Itil (IT Infrastructure Library) = IT Service Management — addresses How IT services are delivered and operated day-to-day. Organizations use both: Cobit for governance, Itil for service delivery operations.

5-5.q: Under SOX Section 404, who is responsible for assessing the effectiveness of Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR)?
o: The external auditor only
The internal audit department only
Management — and the external auditor must attest to the assessment (for large accelerated filers)
The audit committee
5-5.e: SOX 404: Management must establish, maintain, and assess the effectiveness of ICFR in the annual report. For large accelerated filers, the External Auditor must also attest to management's assessment. The framework used must be specified — COSO is the standard. This is one of the most costly but important SOX requirements.

5-5.q: SOX Section 302 requires that:
o: External auditors certify the financial statements
The CEO And CFO personally certify the accuracy and completeness of financial reports
The audit committee reviews all transactions
Management hires external auditors directly
5-5.e: SOX 302: The CEO and CFO must Personally Certify each quarterly and annual report — attesting that the statements fairly present the financial condition, that they have disclosed all significant deficiencies in internal controls, and that no fraud was concealed. Criminal penalties apply for false certifications (Section 906).

5-5.q: Under SOX, the audit committee must be composed of:
o: At least one management representative and four independent directors
All Independent directors — no management members; at least one financial expert required
Majority management with one outside director
Any combination the board chooses
5-5.e: SOX requires All audit committee members to be Independent directors (no current or recent ties to the company as employees or consultants). At least One must qualify as a 'financial expert.' The audit committee oversees the external auditor — not management — to prevent management from pressuring auditors.

5-5.q: Which of the following services is Prohibited for external auditors to provide to their audit clients under SOX?
o: Tax compliance services
Internal audit outsourcing to the audit client
Review of interim financial statements
Agreed-upon procedures engagements
5-5.e: SOX Prohibits external auditors from providing Internal Audit Outsourcing to their audit clients — this would be a self-review threat (auditing work they performed). Other prohibited services: bookkeeping, financial IS design/implementation, appraisal/valuation, actuarial, management/HR functions, legal services. Tax services are generally still allowed.

5-5.q: The lead audit partner rotation requirement under SOX requires rotation every:
o: 3 years
10 years
5 Years — mandatory rotation of the lead audit partner
Every year
5-5.e: SOX requires the Lead Audit Partner to rotate every Five Years (not the entire audit firm). This prevents over-familiarity and relationship-based compromises of independence. The cooling-off period after rotation is also 5 years before the same partner can return to the engagement.

5-5.q: SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley Act) applies Primarily to:
o: All US companies regardless of size
Publicly Traded companies registered with the SEC
Only Fortune 500 companies
All companies including non-profits and governmental entities
5-5.e: SOX applies to Publicly Traded companies registered with the SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission). Private companies are Not required to comply, though many adopt SOX principles as best practice. Foreign private issuers listed on US exchanges are also subject to SOX requirements, though some provisions are modified.

5-5.q: The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) was established by SOX to:
o: Set accounting standards (US GAAP)
Oversee And Regulate Auditors of public companies — set auditing standards and inspect audit firms
Regulate the SEC itself
Set ethics standards for management accountants
5-5.e: PCAOB was created by SOX to: (1) Register public company audit firms; (2) set Auditing Standards for public company audits; (3) Inspect registered audit firms; (4) investigate and Discipline firms and individuals for violations. Before SOX, the accounting profession was self-regulated — the Enron/WorldCom scandals showed this was inadequate.

5-5.q: Under SOX, management's assessment of ICFR in the annual report must reference a recognized control framework. The Most commonly used framework is:
o: Cobit
The COSO Framework — the standard for evaluating ICFR effectiveness
Basel Iii
5-5.e: Management's SOX 404 assessment of Internal Control over Financial Reporting (ICFR) must use a Recognized Framework — the COSO Internal Control Framework (2013) is the standard used by virtually all US public companies. The annual report must name the framework used and management's conclusion on whether ICFR is effective.

5-5.q: When a public company's external auditor issues an Adverse opinion on ICFR, it means:
o: The financial statements are materially misstated
A Material Weakness exists in ICFR — the controls are Not effective
The company has committed fraud
The audit was qualified due to scope limitations
5-5.e: An Adverse opinion on ICFR means the external auditor has concluded that a Material Weakness exists — the internal controls over financial reporting are Not effective. Investors and regulators view this very seriously. The company's stock price often drops significantly, and management credibility is damaged. It signals that the FS may not be reliable.

5-5.q: SOX Section 409 requires companies to disclose material changes to their financial condition or operations:
o: In the annual 10-K filing only
At the next quarterly earnings call
On A Rapid Basis — in real time through current reports (8-K filings)
Only when required by the SEC
5-5.e: SOX Section 409 — Real-Time Disclosure: companies must disclose material changes to their financial condition or operations On A Rapid Basis through current reports (8-K filings). This prevents insider trading advantages and ensures all investors receive material information simultaneously. 'Rapid basis' means as soon as reasonably practicable after occurrence.

5-5.q: The criminal penalties for knowingly certifying false financial reports under SOX Section 906 include:
o: Civil fines up to $1 million only
Up to $5 million fine and/or 20 Years in prison for willful false certification
Suspension of trading privileges only
License revocation only
5-5.e: SOX Section 906: knowingly certifying financial statements that do not comply with requirements = up to $1M fine and/or 10 years. Willfully certifying false statements = up to $5M fine and/or 20 Years in prison. These severe penalties were designed to deter CEOs and CFOs from pressuring auditors or allowing fraudulent reporting.

5-5.q: Under SOX, an audit committee member is considered Independent if they:
o: Own less than 5% of the company's stock
Have worked for the company no more than 3 years ago
Have No current or recent financial, employment, or consulting relationship with the company
Are not related to the CEO
5-5.e: SOX Independence for audit committee members: the director must have No current or recent financial relationship with the company Other than board compensation. This means no current employment, no consulting relationships, no business dealings that could compromise their objectivity. The intent is that audit committee members answer to shareholders, not management.

5-5.q: The 'financial expert' required on the audit committee under SOX must have:
o: A CPA license only
An Mba from an accredited institution
An understanding of GAAP, financial statements, audit standards, and internal controls through relevant experience
At least 20 years of accounting experience
5-5.e: The SOX Financial Expert on the audit committee must have expertise in: (1) GAAP application; (2) preparation or auditing of financial statements of comparable companies; (3) application of accounting principles for estimates, accruals, and reserves; (4) internal controls; (5) functioning of audit committees. The background can come from experience, not necessarily a specific credential.

5-5.q: Under SOX, external auditors are Prohibited from providing which service to their audit clients?
o: Tax return preparation
Reviewing quarterly financial statements
Bookkeeping or accounting services for the audit client
Reviewing the company's accounting policies
5-5.e: SOX Prohibited Services for external auditors: bookkeeping/accounting, financial IS design and implementation, appraisal/valuation, actuarial, internal audit outsourcing, management or HR functions, broker-dealer/investment adviser services, legal services. The key principle: auditors cannot audit their own work (self-review threat). Tax services and reviews of interim statements remain generally permitted.

5-5.q: SOX Section 302 certifications are required:
o: Annually only — with the 10-K filing
Quarterly And Annually — with every 10-Q and 10-K filing
Only when a restatement occurs
Only at the CEO's discretion
5-5.e: SOX 302 certifications are required with Every periodic report — both Annual (10-K) and Quarterly (10-Q). Each filing, the CEO and CFO must personally certify: (1) they have reviewed the report; (2) it does not contain material misstatements; (3) financial statements fairly present the company's condition; (4) they have disclosed all significant control deficiencies and fraud.

5-5.q: Which companies are Exempt from the external auditor attestation requirement under SOX Section 404(b)?
o: All foreign private issuers
Only companies with revenues under $100 million
Non-Accelerated Filers (smaller reporting companies below SEC thresholds) — management assessment still required, but no auditor attestation
All companies incorporated outside the US
5-5.e: SOX 404(b) — external auditor attestation — applies to Large Accelerated Filers and Accelerated Filers. Non-Accelerated Filers (smaller public companies below certain float thresholds) must still comply with 404(a) (management assessment) but are exempt from the Auditor Attestation requirement under 404(b), reducing compliance costs for smaller public companies.

5-5.q: The Cooling-Off Period under SOX before a former audit firm partner can become a client company's CFO is:
o: 2 years
1 Year — the audit firm cannot audit the company if the CEO/CFO worked for the firm in the past year
5 years
3 years
5-5.e: SOX: An audit firm cannot conduct the audit if the company's CEO, CFO, controller, or Cao was Employed By the audit firm and participated in the company's audit within the past One Year. This 'revolving door' restriction prevents close personal relationships between auditors and clients from compromising independence.

5-5.q: Which event Most Directly triggered the passage of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act in 2002?
o: The 2001 dot-com bubble burst
The September 11 terrorist attacks
Major Corporate Accounting Scandals — Enron, WorldCom, Tyco — destroying investor confidence
Rising audit fees charged by Big 4 firms
5-5.e: SOX was a Direct Response to the Enron (energy company — off-balance sheet fraud, $74B loss), WorldCom ($11B accounting fraud), Tyco, and Adelphia scandals. These revealed that existing self-regulatory mechanisms were inadequate. SOX strengthened corporate governance, auditor independence, and management accountability to restore Investor Confidence in US capital markets.

5-5.q: Under SOX, management's responsibility for ICFR includes:
o: Designing controls to prevent all errors and fraud absolutely
Establishing, Maintaining, And Assessing the effectiveness of ICFR — and concluding on its effectiveness in the annual report
Only disclosing weaknesses found by the external auditor
Setting accounting policy — not designing controls
5-5.e: SOX 404(a): Management must: (1) Establish effective ICFR; (2) Maintain it — keep it working; (3) Assess its effectiveness as of year-end; (4) Conclude in the annual report whether ICFR is effective. Management uses the COSO framework for the assessment. If a material weakness exists, management Cannot conclude ICFR is effective.

5-5.q: The Primary purpose of SOX was to:
o: Reduce the cost of capital for US companies
Simplify US GAAP by eliminating complex rules
Restore Investor Confidence in financial reporting by strengthening governance, auditor independence, and management accountability
Harmonize US GAAP with IFRS
5-5.e: SOX's primary purpose was to Restore Investor Confidence in US capital markets after the major corporate scandals of 2001-2002. It achieved this through: strengthening corporate governance (board independence, audit committees), increasing auditor independence (PCAOB oversight, prohibited services), requiring management certification of financial reports, and imposing criminal penalties for fraud.

5-6.q: For true independence, the Chief Audit Executive (Cae) should report Functionally to:
o: The CEO
The CFO
The Audit Committee of the Board
The Controller
5-6.e: The Cae must report Functionally to the Audit Committee (not management) to maintain independence. If the Cae reports only to the CFO or CEO, management could restrict audit scope or suppress unfavorable findings. Administrative reporting to the CEO for day-to-day matters is acceptable, but the Audit Committee must have ultimate oversight.

5-6.q: The Primary purpose of internal auditing is to:
o: Issue an opinion on financial statements for external users
Detect and prosecute all fraud in the organization
Add Value by evaluating and improving risk management, control, and governance
Prepare the company's tax returns
5-6.e: Per the Iia definition, internal auditing adds Value by providing independent, objective assurance and consulting to help organizations achieve their objectives through evaluation of governance, risk management, and control. It is Not limited to fraud detection or financial statement audit — it covers All business operations.

5-6.q: Which of the following is an Assurance engagement (not a consulting engagement) for internal audit?
o: Advising management on designing a new control process
Training the accounting team on IFRS
Evaluating the Effectiveness of existing internal controls over financial reporting
Recommending improvements to the budgeting process
5-6.e: Assurance engagements = objective assessment of a process/control/system — internal audit independently evaluates effectiveness and reports findings. Consulting engagements = advisory services where management requests help designing or improving systems. Key distinction: assurance = evaluating what exists; consulting = advising on what to build/change.

5-6.q: A risk-based audit plan prioritizes audit resources based on:
o: Equal time allocation to all departments
The CFO's personal preferences
Areas with Highest Risk — where controls may be weakest and impact is greatest
Alphabetical order of business units
5-6.e: Risk-Based Audit Planning focuses resources where they matter most: high-risk areas with potentially weak controls and significant consequences. This maximizes the Value of internal audit. The audit plan is based on the organization's risk assessment and must be flexible to respond to emerging risks. Low-risk areas may be audited less frequently.

5-6.q: Internal audit standards are issued by:
o: PCAOB (Public Company Accounting Oversight Board)
FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board)
Iia — Institute of Internal Auditors
Aicpa only
5-6.e: The Iia (Institute of Internal Auditors) issues the International Professional Practices Framework (Ippf), which includes the mandatory Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing. PCAOB sets standards for external auditors of public companies. Aicpa sets standards for external auditors of private companies. FASB sets accounting standards.

5-6.q: The International Professional Practices Framework (Ippf) issued by the Iia includes mandatory guidance consisting of:
o: Optional guidelines and best practices only
The Core Principles, Definition, Code Of Ethics, And Standards — all mandatory for internal auditors
Only the Code of Ethics
Only the Standards for the Professional Practice of Internal Auditing
5-6.e: The Iia's Ippf has two categories: Mandatory guidance (Core Principles, Definition of Internal Auditing, Code of Ethics, and the Standards) which must be followed; and Recommended guidance (Implementation Guidance, Supplemental Guidance) which provides best practices. The mandatory elements define what constitutes professional internal audit practice.

5-6.q: For internal audit to maintain Organizational Independence, the Chief Audit Executive should have:
o: The ability to approve their own audit budget
Unrestricted Access to all records, personnel, and facilities and no operational responsibilities that they would audit
Direct oversight of the accounting department
The authority to hire and fire accounting staff
5-6.e: Organizational Independence requires: (1) Unrestricted Access to all information needed for audits; (2) No Operational Responsibilities in areas being audited (auditing work you control = self-review threat); (3) functional reporting to the Audit Committee (not management). Without independence, internal audit cannot objectively assess controls.

5-6.q: An Operational Audit by the internal audit department evaluates:
o: The accuracy of financial statements
Compliance with tax laws
The Efficiency And Effectiveness Of Operations — whether resources are used optimally to achieve objectives
Only IT system controls
5-6.e: Operational Audit: evaluates the efficiency and effectiveness of operations — are resources (money, people, equipment) being used optimally to achieve organizational objectives? Examples: evaluating procurement processes for cost savings, assessing warehousing efficiency, reviewing the sales process. Much broader than financial audit — focuses on Performance, not just financial accuracy.

5-6.q: An internal Compliance Audit determines whether:
o: Financial statements are fairly stated
Operations are efficient
The Organization IS Adhering to applicable laws, regulations, contracts, and internal policies
Information systems are secure
5-6.e: Compliance Audit: determines adherence to external requirements (laws, regulations, environmental rules, industry standards) and internal policies (code of conduct, operational procedures). Examples: verifying employment law compliance, checking that loan covenants are met, confirming environmental permits are followed. Different from financial audit (FS accuracy) or operational audit (efficiency).

5-6.q: Which of the following Best describes the Three Lines Model in risk management?
o: External audit, internal audit, and the board
First Line: Management owns and manages risks; Second Line: Risk and compliance functions oversee; Third Line: Internal audit provides independent assurance
Operations, finance, and legal departments
Preventive, detective, and corrective controls
5-6.e: Three Lines Model (Iia, updated 2020 — formerly called 'Three Lines of Defense'): (1) First Line — operational management owns risks and implements controls day-to-day; (2) Second Line — risk management and compliance functions oversee first line, provide expertise and monitoring; (3) Third Line — internal audit provides Independent assurance to the board/audit committee on the effectiveness of the first two lines. The 2020 update shifted emphasis from defense to value creation and partnership with the board.

5-6.q: The Internal Audit Charter must include:
o: The detailed audit plan for the coming year
Budget allocations for all audit projects
The Purpose, Authority, And Responsibility of the internal audit function — approved by the audit committee
A list of all past audit findings
5-6.e: The Iia Standards require an Internal Audit Charter that: (1) establishes the internal audit activity's Purpose (why it exists); (2) defines Authority (access rights, ability to report to board); (3) defines Responsibility (what it is accountable for). It must be Approved by the board/audit committee and periodically reviewed. The charter is the foundational document that enables independence.

5-6.q: In the dual reporting structure for internal audit, 'Administrative Reporting' to the CEO means:
o: The CEO controls what internal audit investigates
The CEO can restrict internal audit's access
Day-to-day administrative matters: budget, staffing, compensation — Not audit scope or findings
The CEO approves all internal audit reports before the board sees them
5-6.e: Dual Reporting: Internal audit reports Administratively to the CEO (budget, HR, day-to-day administration) and Functionally to the Audit Committee (audit scope, access, findings, compensation of Cae). This structure maintains independence — the audit committee, not management, has final authority over what internal audit investigates and reports.

5-6.q: A Follow-Up audit by internal audit is performed to:
o: Repeat the same audit procedures from the original engagement
Verify That Management Has Implemented the corrective actions agreed upon after the original audit
Issue a new opinion on the financial statements
Expand the scope of the original audit
5-6.e: Follow-Up audits verify that management has Implemented the corrective actions agreed to in response to original audit findings. Without follow-up, audit findings may never be remediated. Follow-up is a key part of the audit cycle and demonstrates that internal audit drives actual improvement, not just issuing reports. The Iia Standards require internal audit to establish a follow-up process.

5-6.q: Internal audit Adds Value to the organization primarily by:
o: Replacing the external audit and reducing audit fees
Guaranteeing that no fraud will occur in the organization
Providing Independent Assurance And Consulting that improves governance, risk management, and control — helping achieve objectives
Preparing financial statements for management
5-6.e: Per the Iia definition, internal audit Adds Value by providing Independent, Objective assurance and consulting to help the organization achieve its objectives through evaluation and improvement of Governance, Risk Management, and Control processes. It is not just a compliance function — it is a strategic partner that helps the organization improve and manage risk.

5-6.q: An External Quality Assessment of the internal audit function is required by Iia Standards:
o: Every year
Every five years or more frequently — conducted by a qualified independent reviewer from outside the organization
Only when a major fraud is discovered
Voluntarily, with no mandatory frequency
5-6.e: Iia Standards require an External Quality Assessment (Eqa) at least every Five Years by a qualified, independent reviewer (from outside the organization). This 'audit of the auditors' evaluates whether internal audit conforms to Iia Standards and adds value. Organizations use the Eqa conclusion as evidence of internal audit quality to the board and senior management.

5-6.q: When internal audit conducts a Fraud Investigation, the engagement is classified as:
o: An assurance engagement only
A consulting engagement only
A Special Purpose Engagement — involving both assurance elements (gathering evidence) and advisory elements (recommending remediation)
An external audit function
5-6.e: Fraud Investigations are special purpose engagements that combine: (1) Assurance elements — systematically gathering and analyzing evidence to determine what happened, who was involved, and the magnitude; (2) Advisory elements — recommending control improvements to prevent recurrence. The Cae coordinates with legal counsel, HR, and law enforcement as appropriate. Evidence must be handled carefully to preserve chain of custody.

5-6.q: The Scope of internal audit encompasses:
o: Only financial transactions above a materiality threshold
Only compliance with laws and regulations
All Activities That Affect Governance, Risk Management, And Control — operations, finance, compliance, IT, and strategy
Only pre-approved areas in the annual audit plan with no flexibility
5-6.e: Internal audit has a Broad Scope — it can examine Any area of the organization that affects governance, risk management, and control. This includes financial processes, operations, IT systems, compliance, supply chain, strategic initiatives, and culture. The audit plan prioritizes high-risk areas, but the Cae must have authority to investigate any area. Restrictions on scope compromise independence.

5-6.q: Risk-Based Audit Planning means that audit resources are focused on:
o: Equal coverage of all departments on a rotating basis
Areas that management specifically requests be audited
Areas With The Highest Risk — where controls may be weakest and the financial/operational impact of failure is greatest
Areas with the most employees
5-6.e: Risk-Based Audit Planning: internal audit prioritizes the Highest Risk areas — where likelihood of failure is greatest, where controls may be weakest, and where the consequences of failure would be most significant (financial loss, regulatory penalty, reputational damage). This maximizes the value of limited audit resources and ensures audit coverage aligns with actual organizational risk.

5-6.q: The Difference between an internal auditor and an external auditor in terms of independence is that:
o: Internal auditors are more independent than external auditors
External auditors are employees of the company they audit
External Auditors are independent From the company (not employed); internal auditors are independent Within the company (no operational responsibilities in areas they audit)
Both have identical independence requirements
5-6.e: External Auditor independence = independence From the organization — they are completely separate, hired by shareholders/audit committee, and cannot be employees. Internal Auditor independence = organizational independence Within the entity — they are employees but should have no operational responsibilities in areas they audit, report functionally to the audit committee, and maintain objectivity on specific engagements.

5-6.q: According to Iia Standards, internal audit should assess and contribute to improving which three areas?
o: Financial reporting, tax compliance, and audit fees
Revenue recognition, cost accounting, and cash management
Governance, Risk Management, And Control Processes
Operations, marketing, and strategy
5-6.e: The Iia's definition of internal auditing specifically states that internal audit helps organizations achieve objectives by evaluating and improving the effectiveness of: (1) Governance — board and management oversight; (2) Risk Management — identification and management of risks; (3) Control Processes — operations, financial reporting, compliance. These three pillars define internal audit's purpose and scope.

5-7.q: Which type of fraud is the Most Common according to the Acfe (Association of Certified Fraud Examiners)?
o: Financial statement fraud
Corruption
Asset Misappropriation — cash theft, payroll fraud, expense schemes
Tax evasion
5-7.e: Asset Misappropriation is by far the most Common fraud (approximately 89% of cases in Acfe reports) — involving theft or misuse of company assets. Financial statement fraud is the Least common but causes the Most financial damage per case. Corruption falls in between in both frequency and dollar impact.

5-7.q: The Fraud Diamond adds which fourth element to the Fraud Triangle?
o: Detection
Concealment
